topics.name: Linked List
topics.description: It organizes data in nodes linked by pointers to allow constant-time insertions at endpoints and dynamic growth.
topics.designed_for: Dynamic memory and frequent insertions.
topics.purpose: Implementation of stacks, queues, and graph adjacency lists.
topics.how_it_works: Chain of nodes with next-pointers.
takeaways: A Linked List is a **chain of nodes**, each pointing to the next one
The first node is called the **Head** -- it's our entry point
The last node points to **None** -- meaning "the chain ends here"
**Adding** to the chain = create a new node and link it
**Deleting** from the chain = bypass the node by re-linking its neighbors
It's great for **inserting** and **deleting**, but slow for **searching** (you can't jump to the middle!)

topics.name: Stack / Queue
topics.description: It restricts element access to one end (LIFO Stack) or opposite ends (FIFO Queue).
topics.designed_for: Restricted LIFO (Stack) / FIFO (Queue) access.
topics.purpose: Stack: Recursion/Backtracking. Queue: Scheduling/BFS.
topics.how_it_works: Endpoint pushes/pops only.
takeaways: A Stack follows the **LIFO** rule -- Last In, First Out
You can only touch the **TOP** -- no peeking at the middle!
**PUSH** = Add to the top, **POP** = Remove from the top
Items come out in **reverse order** from how they went in
Stacks are used everywhere: Undo history, browser back, function calls
A Queue follows the **FIFO** rule -- First In, First Out
**ENQUEUE** = Join the BACK, **DEQUEUE** = Served from the FRONT
Items come out in the **SAME order** they went in
It's the opposite of a Stack (Stack = reverse order, Queue = same order)
Used everywhere: printers, web servers, customer support lines

topics.name: Stack using Queues
topics.description: Simulates LIFO (Stack) behavior using FIFO (Queue) structures through element rotation or auxiliary storage.
topics.designed_for: Algorithmic thinking and understanding data structure limitations.
topics.purpose: Interview prep and understanding how restricted structures can simulate each other.
topics.how_it_works: Either uses element rotation in a single queue or transfers between two queues during push.
pros: Deeply demonstrates understanding of structure properties.

worst.note: Every push requires rotating all elements.

takeaways: You can build a **Stack** using either **one** or **two** queues.
The "trick" is always to move the **newest** item to the **front** of the queue.
Because Queues only let you take from the front, we must shuffle items during the **Push** operation to keep them in the right order.
While this works perfectly, it is **slower** than a normal stack because of the extra shuffling (O(n) instead of O(1)).

topics.name: Binary Tree
topics.description: A hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children (left and right).
topics.designed_for: Representing hierarchical data and being the base for BSTs/Heaps.
topics.purpose: Organizing data into branches for efficient hierarchical access.
topics.how_it_works: Nodes are connected via edges, forming a parent-child relationship with a single root.
cons: Worst case O(n) for unstructured trees.
takeaways: Each node has a **maximum of 2 children**.
**Height** determines the maximum capacity of the tree.
**Leaf nodes** have no children.
Standard for representing hierarchical data and hierarchical decisions.

topics.name: Strict Binary Tree
topics.description: A binary tree version where every node has either 0 or 2 children, never just one.
topics.designed_for: Optimal encoding (Huffman) and decision trees.
topics.purpose: Ensuring all internal nodes have a full set of children for symmetrical operations.
topics.how_it_works: Enforces a '0 or 2' child rule across all levels.
cons: Requires more nodes than standard trees for same height.
takeaways: No node can have **exactly one child**.
The number of leaf nodes is always one more than the number of internal nodes.
For a given height $H$, it requires **more nodes** than a standard binary tree to remain "Strict" ($2H+1$ vs $H+1$).
Commonly used in **Huffman Coding** and logic-based decision trees.

topics.name: Binary Heap
topics.description: A complete binary tree that maintains the heap property (Min/Max).
topics.designed_for: Fast access to the minimum or maximum element.
topics.purpose: Implementation of Priority Queues and efficient sorting.
topics.how_it_works: Maintains a semi-sorted structure where the parent is always smaller (Min) or larger (Max) than its children.
takeaways: Heaps are **not sorted**; they only guarantee the root is the extreme value.
Implemented using **arrays** for memory efficiency.
**Complete tree structure** ensures the height is always $\log n$.
Standard tool for **Priority Queues** and **HeapSort**.
**Building a heap** from an array takes $O(n)$ time using the bottom-up approach.

topics.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set)
topics.description: It manages disjoint sets by merging them based on rank and using path compression for fast root lookups.
topics.designed_for: Disjoint set tracking and connectivity checking.
topics.purpose: Kruskal's MST, detecting groups in networks.
topics.how_it_works: Find-root and Union-by-rank with path compression.
takeaways: Union-Find tracks **groups** (teams/friend circles)
**FIND** = "Who is the leader of this person's group?"
**UNION** = "Merge these two groups into one"
**Union by Rank** = Always make the smaller group join the bigger one (keeps things fast)
Operations are nearly **instant** -- almost O(1) time!
It's a key building block for many graph algorithms (like Kruskal's MST)

topics.name: Selection Sort
topics.description: It repeatedly searches for the minimum element in the unsorted portion and swaps it to the front.
topics.designed_for: Sorting very small datasets where simplicity and memory-write minimization are prioritized.
topics.purpose: Used in embedded systems or hardware where the cost of writing to memory is significantly higher than reading. It performs exactly O(n) swaps.
topics.how_it_works: Repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted part and swaps it with the first element of that part.
cons: Very slow O(n^2) even for sorted lists

best.note: Always scans the full unsorted list.

takeaways: Selection Sort **scans** the entire unsorted section to find the smallest item
It **swaps** that smallest item to the front of the unsorted section
The sorted section **grows by 1** with each pass
It's **simple to understand** but **slow** for big lists -- O(n)
Best for small lists or when you want a simple, easy-to-code solution

topics.name: Insertion Sort
topics.description: It picks elements one by one and inserts them into their correct sorted position relative to the already-sorted part.
topics.designed_for: Sorting small datasets or lists that are already mostly sorted.
topics.purpose: A foundational algorithm used in hybrid sorts like Timsort. It is excellent for real-time data streaming where items arrive one by one.
topics.how_it_works: Processes elements one by one, finds the correct position in the sorted part and shifts larger elements.

best.note: Occurs when original list is already sorted.

takeaways: Insertion Sort works like **sorting cards in your hand**
Pick each item and **slide it left** until it reaches its correct position
The sorted section grows from left to right
**Best case: O(n)** -- if the list is nearly sorted, it's blazing fast!
**Worst case: O(n)** -- if the list is in reverse order, lots of sliding needed

topics.name: Merge Sort
topics.description: It recursively divides the list into halves, sorts them, and merges them using a sorted combination step.
topics.designed_for: Stable, high-performance sorting for large datasets.
topics.purpose: The standard choice for sorting linked lists and for external sorting where data is too large for RAM.
topics.how_it_works: A Divide and Conquer algorithm. Splits the array into single-element sub-lists, then merges them back together in sorted order.

best.note: Always divides and merges the full tree.

takeaways: **Divide:** Keep splitting the list in half until each piece has 1 item
**Merge:** Combine two sorted pieces into one sorted piece by comparing top items
Time complexity: **O(n log n)** -- much faster than O(n) algorithms
It's like sorting exam papers by splitting the workload among friends
The "merge" step is the real magic -- combining two sorted lists is very efficient

topics.name: Quick Sort
topics.description: It partitions the array around a pivot element so that smaller elements are on the left and larger on the right.
topics.designed_for: Extreme performance general-purpose in-memory sorting.
topics.purpose: Used widely in performance-critical software and standard libraries (like C++ std::sort).
topics.how_it_works: Chooses a pivot and partitions the array into two sub-arrays: smaller than pivot and larger. Recursively sorts them.
cons: Worst case O(n^2) with poor pivot selection

best.note: Pivot always splits the list roughly in half.

worst.note: Unlucky pivot choice (e.g., already sorted data).

takeaways: Quick Sort picks a **Pivot** and puts everything smaller to its left, larger to its right
After partitioning, the Pivot is in its **final correct position**
The process repeats for the left and right groups
**Average case: O(n log n)** -- very fast!
**Worst case: O(n)** -- happens when the pivot is always the smallest or largest
It sorts **in-place** -- no extra memory needed (unlike Merge Sort)

topics.name: Binary Search
topics.description: It repeatedly halves the search space by comparing the target value to the middle element of a sorted list.
topics.designed_for: Instant lookups in sorted datasets.
topics.purpose: The foundation for database indexing and fast search in pre-sorted libraries.
topics.how_it_works: Checks the middle element. Halves the search space every single time based on comparison.
cons: No faster than linear for linked lists.

best.note: Target is exactly at the middle.

worst.note: Target is at the leaves or not found.

takeaways: Binary Search only works on **sorted lists**
It cuts the search area in **half** with every step
Time complexity: **O(log n)** -- incredibly fast even for billions of items
It works like finding a name in a phonebook -- you don't read every page!
Two implementations: **Iterative** (while loop) and **Recursive** (self-calling function)

topics.name: Balanced Binary Search Tree (AVL)
topics.description: It performs tree rotations during insertion and deletion to keep the height difference of subtrees to at most one.
topics.designed_for: Dynamic datasets where both search and insertion must be fast.
topics.purpose: Used in databases and memory managers where data frequently changes. Prevents skewing.
topics.how_it_works: Maintains a strict balance during insertion/deletion using Rotations.
takeaways: A BST stores numbers with **smaller on the left, larger on the right**
If data arrives in order, a normal BST becomes a **straight line** (very slow!)
An **AVL Tree** fixes this by automatically **rotating** whenever it gets unbalanced
**Balance Factor** = Height(Left)  Height(Right), must be -1, 0, or +1
**Rotations** are the self-correcting moves: Left, Right, Left-Right, Right-Left
**Inorder traversal** always gives numbers in **sorted order**
Guaranteed **O(log n)** search time -- even with millions of items!

topics.name: Quick Select / Fast Select
topics.description: It partitions the array like Quick Sort but only recurses into the side containing the desired k-th element.
topics.designed_for: Finding the k-th smallest element (Statistics/Median).
topics.purpose: Used in median algorithms and picking top-k without full sort. Fast Select (Median of Medians) provides a better worst-case guarantee.
topics.how_it_works: Partitions like Quick Sort but only recurses into one half.
pros: Faster than O(n log n) sorting.
cons: Quick Select can be O(n^2); Fast Select is more stable.

worst.note: O(n^2) for QuickSelect; O(n) for FastSelect (Median of Medians).

takeaways: **Efficiency:** Fastest way to find a median or a specific percentile.
**Dividing:** Uses the same "Partition" logic as QuickSort.
**Discarding:** In every step, it narrows down the search space.
**Performance:** Expect linear time ($O(N)$), but watch out for the $O(N^2)$ worst case!
**Real-Life Use:** Finding the median of a dataset, calculating percentiles, and selecting the top $k$ items.

topics.name: Karatsuba's Multiplication
topics.description: It breaks large multiplications into three smaller recursive calls, reducing complexity from O(n^2) to O(n^1.58).
topics.designed_for: Multiplying very large integers.
topics.purpose: Cryptography (RSA) and high-precision calculations.
topics.how_it_works: Breaks multiplication into 3 smaller multiplications (n/2).
takeaways: **Efficiency:** For small numbers, $n^2$ is fine. For million-digit numbers, $n^{1.58}$ is a lifesaver.
**Strategy:** Divide the number into halves, perform 3 recursive multiplications, and combine the results.
**The Trick:** The middle term $ad + bc$ can be calculated from $(a+b)(c+d) - ac - bd$.
**Use-Case:** Large integer math, cryptography (RSA), and scientific simulations.

topics.name: Counting Inversions
topics.description: It counts elements in the right half that are smaller than the left half during the Merge step of a sort.
topics.designed_for: Measuring how unsorted a list is.
topics.purpose: Recommendation engines (comparing user preferences).
topics.how_it_works: Sort and count cross-inversions during the merge step.
takeaways: **Similarity Score:** More inversions = more "opposite" orders.
**Efficiency:** $O(n \log n)$ time is achieved by piggybacking on Merge Sort.
**The Rule:** Every time an element from the right half is placed before elements left in the left half, we add `len(left_remaining)` to our count.
**Use-Case:** Collaborative filtering, gene similarity in biology, and ranking validation.

topics.name: Closest Pair of Points
topics.description: It splits points by x-coordinates, solves for halves, and checks a central strip for pairs closer than current candidates.
topics.designed_for: Finding the 2 closest points in a plane.
topics.purpose: Air traffic control collision detection and graphics proximity checks.
topics.how_it_works: Divide by X-coord, solve halves, check strip near divide.
pros: Much faster than O(n^2) brute force.
takeaways: **Comparison:** Brute force is $O(n^2)$, but Divide & Conquer is $O(n \log n)$.
**Dividing:** Split points by a median line on the x-axis.
**The Strip:** Only check points in the middle that could possibly be closer than our current record.
**Efficiency:** Sorting by Y inside the strip is the "secret sauce" that makes it $O(n \log n)$.
**Real-Life Use:** Air traffic control, pattern recognition, and computer graphics (collision detection).

topics.name: Huffman Encoding
topics.description: It greedily merges the two least frequent characters into a tree to build optimal, variable-length binary codes.
topics.designed_for: Optimal lossless data compression.
topics.purpose: Foundation of ZIP, JPEG, and MP3 formats.
topics.how_it_works: Greedy tree building based on frequency.
takeaways: **Frequent characters** get **short codes**, **rare characters** get **long codes**
Build a tree by repeatedly merging the **two rarest** characters
**LEFT = 0, RIGHT = 1** when reading the tree
Codes are **prefix-free** -- no code starts with another code
Results in **significant space savings** (often 20-50% compression)
The **Heap version** is faster (O(n log n)) for large files

topics.name: Interval Scheduling
topics.description: It greedily selects the task that ends earliest and doesn't overlap with previously chosen tasks.
topics.designed_for: Selecting max number of non-overlapping tasks.
topics.purpose: Resource booking and scheduling systems.
topics.how_it_works: Always pick the task ending earliest.
takeaways: **Sort by end time** (earliest first)
**Pick greedily** -- if a meeting starts after the room is free, take it!
This gives the **maximum number** of non-overlapping meetings
Time complexity: **O(n log n)** (just the sorting step!)
This is mathematically **proven** to be the optimal strategy

topics.name: Minimize Lateness
topics.description: It sorts all tasks by their deadlines and processes them in that order to minimize the maximum lateness.
topics.designed_for: Scheduling tasks to reduce specific delays.
topics.purpose: Industrial scheduling and management fairness.
topics.how_it_works: Sort based on the earliest deadlines first.
takeaways: **Sort tasks by deadline** (earliest first) -- this is the greedy strategy
**Lateness** = Finish Time  Deadline (negative means on time!)
The goal is to minimize the **MAXIMUM** lateness, not the average
This strategy is **mathematically proven** to be optimal
Time complexity: **O(n log n)** -- just the sorting step!
Think of it as: "Handle the most urgent thing first!"

topics.name: BFS / DFS
topics.description: BFS explores levels layer-by-layer using a Queue; DFS explores as deep as possible using a Stack or Recursion.
topics.designed_for: Graph traversal and searching.
topics.purpose: BFS: Unweighted shortest path. DFS: Cycle detection and puzzles.
topics.how_it_works: Queue (BFS) vs Stack/Recursion (DFS) traversal.
cons: BFS has high space for wide graphs.
takeaways: BFS uses a **Queue** (First-In, First-Out)
It explores **layer by layer** -- all neighbors first, then neighbors of neighbors
It guarantees the **shortest path** in unweighted graphs
It visits nodes in **increasing order of distance** from the start
Works like ripples in a pond -- spreading outwards evenly!
DFS uses a **Stack** (Last-In, First-Out) -- or recursion
It explores **as deep as possible** before backtracking
It does **NOT** guarantee the shortest path (unlike BFS)
It's great for **mazes, cycles, and topological sorting**
**Iterative** = manual stack, **Recursive** = function calls itself
DFS visits nodes like exploring a cave -- go deep, then come back and try another tunnel!

topics.name: Topological Sort / Longest Path
topics.description: It iteratively removes nodes with zero in-degree (Kahn's) or uses post-order DFS to determine task dependencies.
topics.designed_for: Linear ordering of tasks in a DAG.
topics.purpose: Critical path analysis in project management.
topics.how_it_works: Uses DFS or Indegree-zero steps (Kahn's). Finds longest path using order.
takeaways: Topological Sort finds a **valid ordering** of tasks with dependencies
It only works on **DAGs** (Directed Acyclic Graphs) -- no circular dependencies!
**In-Degree** = number of prerequisites a task has
Tasks are **ready** when their In-Degree drops to **0**
There may be **multiple valid orderings** -- all are correct!
Used in **course planning, build systems, project management**
Combines **Topological Sort + Dynamic Programming**
The longest path determines the **minimum project completion time**
Uses the formula: `depth[neighbor] = MAX(depth[neighbor], depth[current] + 1)`
**MAX** ensures we always keep the **longest** path (not the shortest!)
A task on the critical path **cannot be delayed** without delaying the whole project
Only works on **DAGs** -- no cycles allowed!

topics.name: Kruskal's Algorithm
topics.description: It first sorts all edges in the graph by weight and greedily adds them if they don't form a cycle (via Union-Find).
topics.designed_for: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) in Sparse Graphs.
topics.purpose: Efficiently connecting all nodes with minimum total edge weight, best for graphs with fewer edges.
topics.how_it_works: Sorts all edges and uses Union-Find to add edges in greedy order while avoiding cycles.
takeaways: **Sort all edges** by cost (cheapest first)
**Add edges** one by one -- skip if it creates a cycle
Uses **Union-Find** to efficiently check for cycles
Stop when you have **n-1 edges** (all nodes connected)
Both Kruskal's and Prim's find the **same MST** -- just different approaches!

topics.name: Prim's Algorithm
topics.description: It greedily selects the minimum edge from the current frontier to connect to an unvisited node (usually via Priority Queue).
topics.designed_for: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) in Dense Graphs.
topics.purpose: Efficiently connecting all nodes with minimum total edge weight, best for graphs with many edges.
topics.how_it_works: Uses a Priority Queue to pick the smallest edge connecting the growing tree to an external vertex.
takeaways: Prim's **grows from a seed node** outward
At each step, it picks the **cheapest edge** connecting the MST to an outside node
It's **Greedy** -- always picks the locally cheapest option
The result has exactly **n-1 edges** (for n nodes)
Total cost is the **minimum possible** to connect all nodes without cycles

topics.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
topics.description: It greedily selects the closest unvisited node and relaxes its outgoing edges (usually via a Priority Queue).
topics.designed_for: Shortest path in weighted graphs (Positive).
topics.purpose: GPS Navigation, Network routing (OSPF).
topics.how_it_works: Greedy exploration with a priority queue.
topics.negatives_behavior: Strictly NO ❌. Cannot handle negative edge weights as they break the greedy assumption.
takeaways: Dijkstra's finds the **shortest path in a weighted graph**
It uses **Relaxation** -- keep updating distances when shorter paths are found
It's **Greedy** -- always processes the cheapest unvisited node
Time complexity: **O((V+E) log V)** with a priority queue
**Cannot handle negative edge weights** -- use Bellman-Ford for that
Used in **GPS navigation, network routing, and game pathfinding**

topics.name: Bellman-Ford
topics.description: It iteratively relaxes all E edges (V-1) times to find shortest paths and detect negative cycles.
topics.designed_for: Weighted shortest path with negative weights.
topics.purpose: Detecting negative cycles and RIP routing.
topics.how_it_works: Relaxes all edges V-1 times; V-th time for cycles.
topics.negatives_behavior: Works with negative edge weights but NOT with negative cycles (it detects the cycle instead).
takeaways: Bellman-Ford relaxes **ALL edges**, repeated **(n-1) times**
It works with **negative edge weights** (unlike Dijkstra!)
It can **detect negative cycles** -- infinite loops with decreasing cost
It's **slower** than Dijkstra but more **versatile**
Why n-1 times? Because the longest simple path has at most n-1 edges

topics.name: Floyd-Warshall
topics.description: It uses dynamic programming to check if every node k can serve as an intermediate shortcut between all pairs (i, j).
topics.designed_for: All-pairs shortest path.
topics.purpose: Network density analysis and connectivity matrices.
topics.how_it_works: DP with a 3D matrix (dist[i][j][k]).
topics.recurrence_formula: SP^k[i, j] = min(SP^(k-1)[i, j], SP^(k-1)[i, k] + SP^(k-1)[k, j])
pros: Finds all paths in one run.
topics.negatives_behavior: Handles negative edge weights but NOT negative cycles.
takeaways: **All-to-All:** A single run gives the distance matrix for the entire graph.
**Logic:** $dist(i,j) = \min(dist(i,j), dist(i,k) + dist(k,j))$.
**The Golden Rule (Negatives):**
**Negative Weights?** YES [YES]! It handles negative paths perfectly.
**Negative Cycles?** NO [NO]. It cannot provide a valid answer if the graph contains a negative total cycle.
**Performance:** $O(V^3)$ is slow for large graphs, so use it when the number of nodes is small (usually less than 500).
**Real-Life Use:** Transitive closure (finding "who knows whom"), network routing, and project scheduling.

topics.name: MST vs Shortest Path
topics.description: A comparative study of Global vs Local connectivity optimization in graphs.
topics.designed_for: Understanding the fundamental choice between total network cost and individual route cost.
topics.purpose: Deciding between MST (e.g., Kruskal/Prim) and Shortest Path (e.g., Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford) for specific engineering problems.
topics.how_it_works: Analyzes the difference between minimizing total edge sum (MST) and minimizing path travel cost (Shortest Path).
takeaways: **MST** connects every node; **Shortest Path** connects a source to a target.
MST minimizes the **sum of all edges**; Shortest Path minimizes the **path length**.
An edge in a Shortest Path is **not guaranteed** to be in an MST (and vice-versa).
**Prim/Kruskal** are the go-to for MST; **Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford** for Shortest Path.
In an MST, there is only **one path** between any two nodes. In a graph, there can be many.

topics.name: Fibonacci (DP Case Study)
topics.description: Explores the transition from slow recursion to optimized Dynamic Programming using the Fibonacci sequence.
topics.designed_for: Understanding memoization, tabulation, and space optimization.
topics.purpose: Learning how to save results to avoid redundant work.
topics.how_it_works: Demonstrates three levels of optimization: Recursion -> Memoization -> Tabulation -> Space-Optimized.
pros: Linear space can often be optimized to constant space.
cons: Can be harder to conceptualize than simple recursion.
takeaways: **Don't Re-calculate:** If you see the same problem twice, save the answer!
**Memoization** is great for problems where you don't need to solve *every* single sub-part.
**Tabulation** is usually faster because it avoids function overhead.
**Space Optimization** can often turn an O(n) space problem into O(1).
**Dynamic Programming** makes the impossible O(2^n) problems possible in O(n) or O(n^2).

topics.name: House Robber (Max Loot)
topics.description: A classic optimization problem where you must decide which houses to rob to maximize profit without triggering adjacent alarms.
topics.designed_for: Understanding constraints, recurrence relations, and space optimization.
topics.purpose: Learning the Rob/Skip decision pattern in DP.
topics.how_it_works: Builds a DP decision tree where each choice at house 'i' depends on houses 'i-1' and 'i-2'.
pros: Can be reduced to constant space O(1).
cons: Requires careful handling of base cases.
Must correctly identify the skip/rob dependency.
takeaways: **Decision Power:** Every DP problem is just a series of choices (Rob or Skip).
**Dependency:** Your current best choice depends on previous best choices.
**Memory Efficiency:** If you only need info from two steps back, use variables.
**Base Cases:** Always handle the simplest scenarios (empty streets) first.

topics.name: Unique Grid Paths
topics.description: Calculate the number of unique ways a robot can travel from top-left to bottom-right of a grid moving only Right or Down.
topics.designed_for: Visualizing 2D DP grids and combinatorial counting.
topics.purpose: Learning how to sum paths from multiple inputs (Above and Left).
topics.how_it_works: Fills a grid where each cell (r, c) = Above + Left. Boundary cells are initialized to 1.
takeaways: **The sum rule:** Paths(r, c) = Paths(Above) + Paths(Left).
**Boundaries:** Top row and left column always have only 1 way.
**Combinatorics:** Visual representation of Pascal's Triangle!
**Space Efficiency:** Since we only look at the row above, we only need $O(n)$ space.

topics.name: Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)
topics.description: Find the longest sequence of characters that appear in the same order in both strings, regardless of their adjacent positions.
topics.designed_for: Understanding global alignment and sequence similarity.
topics.purpose: Learning the Match/Skip-one-from-either logic.
topics.how_it_works: Builds a DP matrix to find character matches.
takeaways: **Subsequence vs Substring:** Subsequences don't need to be contiguous.
**The decision:** If characters match, add 1. If not, take the MAX of alternatives.
**DNA Matching:** Core algorithm used in genetic sequencing.
**Space Optimization:** Only need current and previous rows.

topics.name: Longest Common Subword (LCW)
topics.description: Find the length of the longest contiguous piece of text present in two strings, highlighting the streak-based logic of substrings.
topics.designed_for: Understanding contiguous alignment and streak resets.
topics.purpose: Learning the Match/Reset-to-Zero logic.
topics.how_it_works: Builds a DP matrix tracking contiguous match streaks.
takeaways: **The streak meter:** If characters don't match, the streak resets to 0.
**Contiguous match:** Used for plagiarism detection and exact matching.
**Global Max:** Must track the maximum value found anywhere in the table.
**Subword = Substring:** Terms used interchangeably in theory.

topics.name: Quiz 1 - Practice Problems
topics.description: A comprehensive set of practice questions covering function analysis, sorting logic, and linked list manipulations.
topics.designed_for: Exam preparation and conceptual reinforcement.
topics.purpose: To test understanding of core Python DSA concepts through manual tracing.
topics.how_it_works: Step-by-step solutions to 18 high-fidelity quiz problems with teacher's notes.
pros: Clear ASCII diagrams for data structures.
takeaways: Mastering **manual tracing** of code is essential for exam success.
Selection Sort condition counts reveal the inner mechanics of the algorithm.
Two-pointer techniques in Linked Lists (Fast/Slow) are powerful for finding midpoints.
Hash collisions and Linear Probing follow a systematic index-shift pattern.
Unique Topological Sorts in DAGs imply the existence of a Hamiltonian Path.
Binary Search logic (searching right for last occurrence) is key for finding bounds.
Adjacency Matrix DFS costs $O(n^2)$ due to mandatory $O(n)$ degree scans per vertex.
